query: effectiveness of cariprazine in treating bipolar disorder

retrieved_docs.title: Bipolar Disorder -- Treatment / Management -- Manic Episode
retrieved_docs.content: The patient’s current medications must be considered. For example, a second drug is recommended if the patient presents while the condition is already managed with lithium monotherapy. Also, antidepressants are usually tapered and discontinued in a manic phase. First-line monotherapy includes a mood stabilizer, such as lithium or valproate, or an antipsychotic, such as aripiprazole, asenapine, cariprazine, quetiapine, or risperidone.

retrieved_docs.title: Mania -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Mania in bipolar I disorder was first treated with medications such as lithium, valproic acid, and carbamazepine. The treatments were focused on mood stabilizers and anticonvulsants which had shown efficacy in mood stabilization. Today, the class of mood stabilizers subsumes more than just lithium and antiepileptics--it includes many second-generation neuroleptics as well. A large metanalysis of medications used in acute mania showed that atypical antipsychotics were more effective than mood stabilizers for this purpose but not necessarily for maintenance of bipolar disorder. Examples of effective medications are risperidone, olanzapine, and haloperidol. Lithium, quetiapine, and aripiprazole were comparatively effective. Valproic acid, carbamazepine, and ziprasidone were more efficacious than placebo but less so than their previously mentioned competitors. Gabapentin, lamotrigine, and topiramate showed no difference when compared to placebo for treating mania. Clozapine and electroconvulsive therapy have shown many benefits for treatment-resistant mania but are less commonly used. Lastly, psychoeducation and psychotherapy are powerful long-term tools for patients with bipolar disorder as well as for their families or caregivers.

retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Antipsychotic Agents -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The FDA has approved several atypical antipsychotic agents. These medications are available in various formulations, including oral and long-acting intramuscular (IM) formulations. Specific formulations may differ depending on the indication. Please refer to individual product labeling for detailed information on formulations according to indications. Aripiprazole: Bipolar I disorder, schizophrenia, irritability associated with autistic disorder, treatment-resistant major depressive disorder, Tourette disorder [4] [5] Asenapine: Bipolar I disorder, schizophrenia [6] Asenapine transdermal: Schizophrenia [7] Cariprazine: Bipolar I disorder, major depressive disorder, schizophrenia [8] Clozapine: Schizophrenia (treatment-resistant), suicidal behavior in schizophrenia or schizoaffective disorder [9] Iloperidone: Schizophrenia [10] Lurasidone: Bipolar I disorder, schizophrenia [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Chlorpromazine -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The efficacy of chlorpromazine in bipolar disorder was mainly established to control the manic episode of bipolar illness, such as excessive energy, decreased need for sleep, increased excitability and impulsivity, and grandiose ideations. Chlorpromazine is the Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved treatment for persistent singultus, a medical problem where hiccupping lasts more than 48 hours. Regarding acute psychosis, studies have shown that chlorpromazine has been effective as a short-term treatment in controlling combativeness and aggressive behavior in children. [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Lurasidone -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Lurasidone is a medication used in the treatment of schizophrenia and bipolar depression. It is in the benzisothiazole class of medications. This activity describes the indications, action, and contraindications for lurasidone as a valuable agent in treating schizophrenia and bipolar depression. This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, and other key factors, e.g., off-label uses, dosing, pharmacodynamics, pharmacokinetics, monitoring, and relevant interactions pertinent for members of the interprofessional team in the treatment of patients with schizophrenia and bipolar depression.

retrieved_docs.title: Mood Stabilizers -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the mechanism of action of mood stabilizers in the treatment of bipolar disorder. Describe the potential adverse effects of mood stabilizers. Review the appropriate follow-up monitoring for mood stabilizers. Summarize the importance of collaboration and communication amongst the interprofessional team to improve outcomes for patients receiving treatment with mood stabilizers. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Mood Stabilizers -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Mood stabilizers are a class of medications used in the management and treatment of bipolar disorder. This activity outlines the indications, action, and contraindications for mood stabilizers as valuable agents in managing and treating bipolar disorder. This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, and other key factors pertinent to members of the healthcare team in the management and treatment of patients with bipolar disorder and related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Bipolar Disorder -- Treatment / Management -- Hypomanic Episodes
retrieved_docs.content: By definition, hypomanic episodes are not severe enough to cause marked impairment, and there is no psychosis; therefore, these episodes can be managed in an ambulatory setting. Pharmacotherapy is similar to that for mania, but higher doses may be required for the latter.

retrieved_docs.title: Bipolar Disorder -- Treatment / Management -- Maintenance Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Most patients with bipolar disorder will require maintenance treatment for many years, possibly lifelong, to prevent recurrent episodes and restore their pre-illness functioning. The current recommendation is for continuous rather than intermittent treatment, with treatments that were effective during the acute phase often continued initially to prevent early relapse. Mood stabilizers and atypical antipsychotics alone or in combination are the mainstays of maintenance pharmacotherapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Fluphenazine -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Although frequently prescribed off-label for psychosis or psychotic symptoms related to the major neurocognitive disorder and related dementing illnesses, all antipsychotics (including fluphenazine) are not FDA-approved for these purposes, and clinicians should avoid their use in such cases. Fluphenazine is not FDA-approved for augmentation of mood stabilization or treating concomitant psychotic symptoms in patients with bipolar disorder. Fluphenazine has also been used off-label to manage chronic tic disorders and Huntington disease to control abnormal movements and chorea.

retrieved_docs.title: Ziprasidone -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify indications for ziprasidone therapy, including schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and acute agitation. Screen patients for potential contraindications and risk factors before initiating ziprasidone treatment. Implement appropriate dosing strategies based on patient-specific factors, such as age, weight, and renal function. Collaborate with interdisciplinary healthcare team members to ensure comprehensive patient care and monitoring. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Bipolar Disorder -- Treatment / Management -- Acute Bipolar Depression
retrieved_docs.content: For patients not already taking long-term medication for BD, first-line monotherapy includes quetiapine, olanzapine, or lurasidone (has not been studied in acute bipolar mania). Combination treatment with olanzapine-fluoxetine, lithium plus lamotrigine, and lurasidone plus lithium or valproate may also be considered.

retrieved_docs.title: Ziprasidone -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Oral: Oral ziprasidone is indicated to treat schizophrenia in adults. [4] Ziprasidone hydrochloride capsules are indicated as monotherapy for adult patients with bipolar I disorder. As an adjunct to lithium or valproate, ziprasidone hydrochloride capsules are used as maintenance therapy for adult patients with bipolar I disorder. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Bipolar Disorder -- Treatment / Management -- Maintenance Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to the individualized pharmacotherapy plan, essential components of maintenance treatment include medication adherence, primary prevention and treatment for psychiatric and medical comorbidities, and psychotherapy when appropriate. Suicidality surveillance is critical throughout the maintenance phase.

retrieved_docs.title: Lurasidone -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Lurasidone is clinically beneficial in treating patients with schizophrenia and bipolar depression. Approximately 80% of the patients with schizophrenia achieve optimal response on 40 to 80 mg of lurasidone per day. Lurasidone is effective in treating depressive symptoms in schizophrenic patients; this is very important as the depressed mood correlates with suicide in these patients. [17] Additional clinical trials are necessary to prove the antisuicidal effects of lurasidone.

retrieved_docs.title: Bipolar Disorder -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Although numerous clinical practice guidelines exist for the treatment and management of bipolar disorder, there is not enough consistency to generate a ‘meta-consensus’ model. [23] Authors of a recent systematic review concluded, “The absence of a uniform language and recommendations in current guidelines may be an additional complicating factor in the implementation of evidence-based treatments in BD.” [24] The following is an abbreviated synthesis of guidelines published by the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE), British Association for Psychopharmacology, International College of Neuro-Psychopharmacology (CINP), Canadian Network for Mood and Anxiety Treatments (CANMAT), International Society for Bipolar Disorders (ISBD), and Indian Psychiatric Society (IPS). [25] [26] [27] [28] [29]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1493
retrieved_docs.content: Antipsychotics are a class of medications employed to treat psychotic disorders such as schizophrenia and bipolar mania. Acepromazine is an example of a drug that belongs to the antipsychotic class, often used for its tranquilizing effects. In contrast, ondansetron is a serotonin 5‑HT₃ receptor antagonist that is used primarily to prevent nausea and vomiting and is not classified as an antipsychotic. This distinction clarifies that while acepromazine functions as an antipsychotic, ondansetron serves a different therapeutic purpose.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1417
retrieved_docs.content: Benperidol, chlorpromazine, levomepromazine maleate, methotrimeprazine hydrochloride, and prochlorperazine mesylate are all medications that may be used to treat اضطراب ذُهاني (psychotic disorder). These agents, which belong to the class of typical antipsychotics, share the therapeutic purpose of alleviating the symptoms of psychosis, such as delusions, hallucinations, and thought disorganization. Their use in clinical practice reflects the broader strategy of employing dopamine‑blocking drugs to manage psychotic conditions, and selecting among them can be guided by factors such as side‑effect profile, patient tolerance, and specific clinical presentation. Effective treatment with any of these drugs can improve patient outcomes by reducing psychotic symptoms and enhancing functional stability.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2595
retrieved_docs.content: Benperidol, chlorpromazine, levomepromazine maleate, prochlorperazine mesylate, and trifluoperazine are all pharmacologic agents that may be used to treat اضطراب ذُهاني (psychotic disorder). These drugs belong to the class of typical antipsychotics and exert their therapeutic effect primarily through antagonism of dopamine D₂ receptors, thereby reducing the positive symptoms of psychosis such as delusions and hallucinations. Chlorpromazine, the first widely used antipsychotic, and its related compounds levomepromazine maleate and prochlorperazine mesylate share similar mechanisms and are employed in both acute and chronic management of psychotic states. Benperidol and trifluoperazine, also dopamine antagonists, are utilized when additional potency or specific side‑effect profiles are desired. The inclusion of these agents in treatment regimens underscores the importance of selecting an appropriate antipsychotic based on efficacy, tolerability, and patient‑specific factors to achieve optimal control of psychotic symptoms and improve overall patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2210
retrieved_docs.content: Mental health behavior encompasses the broad therapeutic category of antidepressants, which serves as a parent class for a variety of pharmacologic agents. Within this class, esketamine is identified as a specific antidepressant, reflecting its role as an NMDA‑receptor antagonist used for rapid‑acting treatment of depressive episodes. Additional compounds classified as antidepressants include sulpiride, clorgyline, moclobemide, nialamide, and rolipram, representing diverse mechanisms such as atypical antipsychotic activity, monoamine‑oxidase inhibition, and phosphodiesterase inhibition. Lithium is listed as a child of the antidepressant category, highlighting its frequent use in the management of mood disorders and its adjunctive role in depressive treatment despite being primarily a mood stabilizer. Lamotrigine is associated with the therapeutic class of antidepressants, underscoring its common use as an adjunctive agent for depressive symptoms in bipolar disorder. Together, these relationships illustrate the interconnected nature of antidepressant therapy, the variety of agents that fall under its umbrella, and the overlap with other mood‑stabilizing medications that contribute to comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1359
retrieved_docs.content: Antidepressants are a pharmacologic group situated within the broader domain of Mental health.behavior, encompassing a variety of agents used to treat mood and depressive disorders. In this classification, specific compounds such as esketamine, casopitant, sulpirid, klorgylin, moklobemid, nialamid, and rolipram are each regarded as instances of antidepressants. The hierarchy also associates lithium and valproát sodný (sodium valproate) with the antidepressant family, indicating that these mood‑stabilizing drugs are sometimes considered child agents or therapeutic subclasses of antidepressants because of their application in depressive and bipolar conditions. Together, this network of entities illustrates the diverse therapeutic landscape that links traditional antidepressants with certain mood‑stabilizing medications under the umbrella of mental health behavior treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1353
retrieved_docs.content: Promazine is classified as a dopamine antagonist, meaning it belongs to the group of agents that block dopamine receptors. As a member of this pharmacologic class, promazine exerts its effects by inhibiting dopaminergic neurotransmission, a mechanism commonly utilized in the treatment of psychiatric disorders such as schizophrenia and in managing certain types of nausea. Understanding that promazine functions as a dopamine antagonist helps clinicians anticipate its therapeutic actions as well as potential side effects related to dopamine blockade, such as extrapyramidal symptoms, sedation, and anticholinergic effects. This classification informs appropriate patient care decisions, including drug selection, dosing, and monitoring for adverse reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1511
retrieved_docs.content: Benperidol, chlorpromazine, and methotrimeprazine hydrochloride are antipsychotic medications that may be used to treat psychotic disorder (اضطراب ذُهاني). Conversely, meflochin and mefloquine hydrochloride are contraindicated in patients with psychotic disorder (اضطراب ذُهاني), indicating that these agents should be avoided because they can exacerbate the condition or pose safety risks. This distinction highlights the need for careful drug selection when managing individuals with psychotic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1463
retrieved_docs.content: Antidepressants are a central pharmacologic group within the domain of mental health behavior, serving as a foundational category for the treatment of behavioral and mood disorders. This class encompasses a variety of specific agents, including esketamine, sulpiride, clorgyline, moclobemide, nialamide, and rolipram, each of which is classified as an antidepressant. Although lithium is listed as a child of antidepressants, it is more accurately described as a mood‑stabilizing agent that is frequently used in bipolar disorder, illustrating the close therapeutic relationship between antidepressant and mood‑stabilizer medications. Similarly, the therapeutic class “sodá sůl kyselí valproové” (the sodium salt of valproic acid, or valproate) is associated with antidepressants; valproate is primarily an anticonvulsant and mood stabilizer but shares pharmacologic overlap with antidepressant treatment strategies. Together, these relationships highlight the interconnected nature of antidepressant drugs, mood stabilizers, and broader mental‑health interventions, emphasizing the importance of integrated pharmacologic management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 468
retrieved_docs.content: Antidepressants are a therapeutic class within the broader domain of mental health behavior. Several specific agents are classified as antidepressants, including casopitant, esketamine, sulpirid, klorgylin, moklobemid, nialamid, rolipram, sertralin, and tofisopam. Lithium is considered a child concept of antidepressants, reflecting its close pharmacological relationship to mood‑modifying therapies. The drugs bupropion hydrobromide and bupropion hydrochloride are also placed in the antidepressant therapeutic class. Both forms of bupropion are contraindicated in patients who have the withdrawal syndrome (متلازمة الانسحاب), indicating that they should not be used in this clinical context. Conversely, tofisopam is noted as a medication that may be used to treat withdrawal syndrome, suggesting a potential therapeutic role for managing this condition. Together, these relationships delineate the categorization of antidepressant agents, their interconnections with related compounds such as lithium, and important safety considerations regarding contraindications and possible treatment applications for withdrawal syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1184
retrieved_docs.content: Antipsychotics are a class of psychotropic medications used to manage conditions such as schizophrenia and bipolar disorder, and they are distinct from ondansetron, which is a serotonin 5‑HT₃ receptor antagonist employed primarily to prevent nausea and vomiting. A recognized long‑term adverse effect of antipsychotic therapy is the development of tardive movement disorders, referred to in Arabic as “خلل الحركة المتأخر.” This connection highlights the need for careful monitoring of patients receiving antipsychotics for extrapyramidal side effects and differentiating these agents from unrelated drugs like ondansetron.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 1164
retrieved_docs.content: Methotrimeprazin and its hydrochloride salt are indicated for the treatment of both اضطراب ذُهاني (psychotic disorder) and اضطراب القلق (anxiety disorder). Oxazepam is used to manage اضطراب القلق but is contraindicated in patients with اضطراب ذُهاني, reflecting the need to avoid benzodiazepine therapy in psychotic conditions. The antipsychotic agents benperidol, chlorpromazin, levomepromazine maleate, prochlorperazine mesylate, trifluoperazin, and trifluoperazine hydrochloride may treat اضطراب ذُهاني and are also listed as therapeutic options for اضطراب القلق, highlighting their dual utility in managing psychosis‑related anxiety symptoms. Conversely, the antimalarial drugs meflochin and mefloquine hydrochloride are contraindicated in both اضطراب ذُهاني and اضطراب القلق, indicating that they should be avoided in patients with either psychotic or anxiety disorders. Clinical observations associate اضطراب ذُهاني with تعاطي المارجوانا (marijuana use), suggesting that cannabis consumption may be a contributing factor or comorbid condition in the presentation of psychotic disorders. These relationships underscore the importance of selecting appropriate pharmacologic agents based on the specific psychiatric diagnosis and avoiding drugs that are contraindicated in the presence of psychosis or anxiety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1394
retrieved_docs.content: Oxazepam is contraindicated in patients with psychotic disorder (اضطراب ذُهاني). In contrast, several agents are considered therapeutic options for this condition: chlorpromazine, levomepromazine maleate, prochlorperazine mesylate, and trifluoperazine may each be used to treat psychotic disorder (اضطراب ذُهاني).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 50
retrieved_docs.content: drugs belong to multiple therapeutic categories—psychostimulants, nootropics, anti‑dementia agents, antidepressants, mood stabilizers, and antipsychotics—and recognizing their shared mechanisms, subclass memberships, and contraindications is essential for safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 1033
retrieved_docs.content: Oxazepam and mefloquine hydrochloride are contraindicated in individuals diagnosed with اضطراب ذُهاني, indicating that these agents should be avoided in the management of this psychotic disorder. In contrast, benperidol, chlorpromazine, and prochlorperazine mesylate are identified as therapeutic options that may be used to treat اضطراب ذُهاني, highlighting their potential role in alleviating the symptoms of this mental health condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 51
retrieved_docs.content: these hierarchical classifications, pharmacodynamic properties, and contraindications is essential for safe prescribing, prevention of harmful drug interactions, and effective management of psychiatric and withdrawal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 614
retrieved_docs.content: Oxazepam is indicated for the treatment of اضطراب القلق (anxiety disorder) but is contraindicated in patients with اضطراب ذهاني (psychotic disorder). Both meflochin and mefloquine hydrochloride are contraindicated in the presence of اضطراب ذهاني and also contraindicated in اضطراب القلق, indicating that these antimalarial agents should be avoided in individuals with either psychotic or anxiety disorders. Benperidol, chlorpromazin, levomepromazine maleate, methotrimeprazine hydrochloride, prochlorperazine mesylate, trifluoperazin, and trifluoperazine hydrochloride are each reported to have therapeutic potential for both اضطراب ذهاني and اضطراب القلق, suggesting that these antipsychotic and phenothiazine derivatives may be employed in the management of psychotic as well as anxiety symptoms. The overall pattern highlights that while several typical antipsychotics and related agents can be used across both disorder categories, certain drugs such as oxazepam, meflochin, and mefloquine hydrochloride have specific contraindications that limit their use in patients with psychotic or anxiety conditions.
